posts.caption:
At the heart of @petrafcollins’ practice is a fascination with the fantasies and fears that shape young womanhood, both online and off.⁠
⁠
Her seventh book – and third with @rizzolibooks – Star follows a fictional pop girl group, tracing the point at which devotion slips into something darker. With chapters titled after songs by Dido, Britney Spears and The Cardigans, its saccharine early pages are charged with the energy of the 2000s without being expressly placed there. ⁠
⁠
Her most ambitious narrative project to date, Collins has described the book as a rehearsal for her first feature film, complete with an original score by composer Jake Nadrich to accompany its reading. This is what practice looks like when you’re Petra Collins.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, she speaks to @orlabeee about fame, fandom and why she’s now turning to film 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Star by Petra Collins. Photography by #PetraCollins

Wearing @isseymiyakeofficial, @solangeknowles – our annniversary issue cover star – arrived last night at a black-tie gala marking one hundred years of the @schomburgcenter for Research in Black Culture, where she was awarded a Schomburg Medal of Excellence. ⁠
⁠
A cornerstone of @nypl, the centre is dedicated to collecting, preserving and making accessible materials that document the global Black experience, from rare archives and manuscripts to exhibitions, research and public programmes. It’s a mission that mirrors Knowles’ own work: through @saintheron, her multidisciplinary platform and archive, she has spent the past decade building spaces for Black cultural memory. ⁠
⁠
“A lot of the questions I’ve been asking myself lately surround the urgency of Black women’s history. It almost feels like survival,” Solange told AnOther Magazine in our latest issue. Revisit the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 by @andymartinezx1

posts.caption:
@lottaantonssonstudio was introduced to collage as a child in the 1960s, scouring magazines her mother brought home from the hospital, where she worked nights as a nurse.⁠
⁠
In I Am Everything at @fotografiska.stockholm, Antonsson takes those formative years and her personal archive of vintage images, collected between 1968 and 1978, as her starting point. Rather than reduced to photogenic objects, Antonsson’s subjects – usually young, “nude” and in repose – accrue layers. Gemstones, crystals and seashells are applied to their eyes, mouths and breasts in a bid to divert or return the male gaze.⁠
⁠
“I want to lure you in with beauty, and then you find something that is compulsive, or confronting or scary,” Antonsson tells @rosehighamstainton. Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
In a new exhibition at @donald_ryan_gallery in New York, photographer @devclaro shares his latest body of work, Crushing, a series of carefully staged photos that depict his version of America, shot in the city and its surrounding outer boroughs – in parking lots, on street corners, surrounded by municipal buildings and civic bridges.⁠
⁠
Claro’s outer borough New York is neither utopian or dystopian. It’s not romanticised and it’s not documentarian. In many ways it’s not even America. “Even if there’s still conflict, poverty, racism, and ritual too, I can’t claim to examine the rest of the country, because my America is a micro-pocket,” he tells @rose_dodd. ⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Happy birthday @kirstendunst, who turns 44 years old today. ⁠
⁠
The actress has made a very public journey from dimpled child walking the red carpet in knee-high socks and jellies via the American sweetheart whose sunny exterior was undercut by choppier waters, to today’s versatile, virtuoso performer with a streak of auteur-driven, break-your-heart performances that hinge on the cartography of her face. “I grew up as I was doing this – I didn’t know what I liked in film as a kid,” she told AnOther. “I had to figure out my own taste, learn all my lessons along the way.” ⁠
⁠
Revisit the interview at the link in bio 📲 ⁠

posts.caption:
Jeremy Pope wore a “Slave to Love” corset jacket from Vivienne Westwood’s Autumn/Winter 1996–97 MAN collection, styled by Law Roach (@luxurylaw). It is one of two evening jackets Vivienne and Andreas commissioned from Mr Pearl, the legendary British corset-maker. Its twin, the “Martyr to Love”, is featured in The Metropolitan Museum of Art’s Spring 2026 exhibition, Costume Art.
The AW96 corset jacket sits at the intersection of three obsessions: Vivienne Westwood’s fascination with 18th-century dress, the radical body discipline of Mr Pearl, and a ’90s moment when fashion was actively rewriting gender codes.
As Jeremy Pope beautifully put it:“Intentionally crafted and designed by hand, it carries history, both beautiful and painful. It feels like my personal artist thesis statement, a visual currency for how I’ve learned to walk in my truth as a Black man, making space for my lineage that is rich, strong, and resilient. I wear it to honor that fullness.”
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.

posts.caption:
For decades, India’s craft traditions have quietly shaped the language of global luxury, often referenced, occasionally replicated, but rarely centred. At the Met Gala 2026, that dynamic felt notably different, as Indian craft emerged not merely as embellishment, but as art in its own right. From Diya Mehta Jatia (@dmjatia) in Mayyur Girotra (@mayyurgirotra)’s revival of Bengal’s Shola craft and Karan Johar’s painterly Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05)’s moment to Sudha Reddy (@sudhareddy.official)’s Kalamkari-inspired couture and Rihanna (@badgalriri)’s Gem Palace jewels, the red carpet became a powerful expression of India’s heritage, artistry, and evolving global design language.
Tap the link in bio to read the full story.
✍️: Santya Ahuja (@santyaahuja)
📸: Getty Videos and Images, Pr Pundit
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia #MetGala2026

posts.caption:
The Met Gala embraced this year’s “Costume of Art” theme, delivering some of the most unforgettable looks of the year. From Emma Chamberlain (@emmachamberlain) in a sculptural Mugler (@muglerofficial) moment to Hudson Williams(@hudsonbwilliams) in Balenciaga (@balenciaga), Rihanna (@badgalriri) in Maison Margiela (@maison) alongside A$AP Rocky (@asaprocky) in Chanel (@chanelofficial) redefining power couple dressing, and supermodel Anok Yai (@anokyai) in a custom look designed by Pierpaolo Piccioli (@pppiccioli), the red carpet was a study in fashion as storytelling. This year’s best dressed list was less about trends and more about transformation, where every look felt like a piece of living art on the Met steps.
📸: Getty Videos and Images
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia #MetGala2026

Ananya Birla (@ananyabirla) and Isha Ambani’s Met Gala looks feature select elements shaped by the Indian artist Subodh Gupta (@subodhguptastudio), bringing a distinct layer of Indian craftsmanship into both ensembles. These sculptural interventions add depth and contrast within each couture narrative without overwhelming the overall design. Indian craftsmanship is thus positioned on a global platform through quiet but powerful design gestures. There is a subtle continuity in the artistic language that runs across both looks. Together, the appearances reflect how contemporary couture can seamlessly integrate artistic expression into high fashion storytelling.

Between walking runways and fronting campaigns internationally to her silver screen debut in ‘Anyone but You’, @charleefraser is a multi-talented woman. But among Fraser’s manifold skills is one talent that she has, until now, kept on the quiet. “I love roller skating,” she shares. “I’m constantly thinking with the work that I do and [my] schedule. I’m a major organiser – such a Capricorn,” she laughs. “[With] my skates on, the only thing I can think about is what I’m doing. And it just really grounds me, brings me back into that present.”

“Oh, man, little baby Charlee,” @charleefraser chuckles as she reflects on her career beginnings. “It was really interesting because I wasn’t looking to model at the time – the opportunity kind of presented itself,” she says of her beginning. By 2016, Fraser had been modelling locally for three years, with early gigs including runway appearances for David Jones, Carla Zampatti and Bec + Bridge. “My agency was like, ‘I think it’s time for you to go overseas. You’ve done everything here. Go spread the wings’,” she says. “At the time, I really felt ready for that.”

posts.caption:
Writer, academic and activist Sinéad Burke (@thesineadburke) has donated two pieces from her private wardrobe to the permanent collection of the Met Museum’s Costume Institute, including a custom-made Burberry trench coat, scissored and shot by Tim Walker (@timwalker) for the cover of BoF’s ‘The Age of Influence’ special print issue in 2018.
The trench appears in the department’s latest exhibition, ‘Costume Art,’ which was unveiled with last night’s Met Gala and is, in part, organised around a series of thematic body types, including ‘the disabled body.’ Burke’s own body was scanned to develop the mannequin on which the coat is displayed.
“This is an explicit and radical invitation for disabled people to see themselves in fashion, to see themselves in the Met, to see themselves at the Costume Institute,” said Burke, who consulted on the exhibition.
Watch how the first mannequin of Burke’s body was created on The BoF Show’s third episode “Everybody is Different. Why Does Fashion Always Look the Same?” #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Anna Wintour’s nearly 30 years leading the Met Gala — the annual fundraiser for the Metropolitan Museum of Art’s Costume Institute — has been an effort to prove fashion’s legitimacy as an art form.
This year, the Costume Institute declared that milestone accomplished, opening its new permanent home inside the museum, the Condé M. Nast Galleries, a 10,000 square-foot space located just off the Great Hall. Beyond the new galleries, this year’s Met Gala raised a record $42 million.
Just a few weeks ago, though, the Met Gala’s success didn’t seem certain.
BoF’s Joan Kennedy (@joooankennedyyy) explores how the event overcame the backlash against sponsors Jeff and Lauren Sánchez Bezos to pull off a record-breaking night. Read more #linkinbio
📷 Getty Images, @joooankennedyyy

posts.caption:
For four decades, Dries Van Noten defined a singular path in global fashion with a universe rooted in intellectual rigour, exquisite craftsmanship and independence. When he stepped back from his eponymous brand last year, it wasn’t a retreat into a quiet retirement. Instead, Van Noten has embarked on a profound transition — moving from the relentless, dictated rhythm of fashion to a new life as a custodian of culture in Venice.
Van Noten has established a new foundation (@fondazionedriesvannoten) at the Palazzo Pisani Moretta, a space dedicated to the beauty of craftsmanship and the belief that in a world marked by global uncertainty, the act of making something beautiful is the ultimate form of protest.
In this special episode of The BoF Podcast, our editor-at-large Tim Blanks (@timblanks) speaks to Dries Van Noten about this remarkable transition to becoming a custodian of beauty.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
📷 Fe Pinheiro

posts.caption:
As the initial results of the creative change sweeping the fashion industry have begun to trickle in, Matthieu Blazy’s Chanel revamp cemented its position at the front of the pack this week, topping the revamped Lyst Index, a measure of brand heat.
Since its October reveal, the style reboot at the French fashion giant has stood out amid the sector-wide reset, dominating discussions online and sparking shopping frenzies in boutiques.
BoF's Mimosa Spencer explores whether the popularity of Blazy’s revamp raises questions about brand heat — and the risk of a rapid cool-down if “Blazymania” goes unmanaged. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @chanelofficial

But could Blazymania go too far?'.

posts.caption:
Last night, guests descended on House of Koko in London for an intimate community dinner co-hosted by BoF and luxury fragrance house @AmouageOfficial. Convening a curated group of leading voices across fashion, art and culture, the evening fostered a nuanced exploration of luxury’s evolving language — and the increasing role of sensorial experiences in shaping memory, identity and desire.
Set within the private members’ spaces, the dinner gathered founders, creative directors and cultural leaders — from @harris_reed and @adotthegreatt to @tishweinstock, @robertwun and @biancasaunders — for dynamic, cross-sector dialogue and open creative exchange.
Against a backdrop of shifting consumer expectations and a renewed focus on emotional storytelling, conversations examined how to cultivate deeper resonance beyond products, and what it means to create enduring value.

posts.caption:
This week in fashion: Adidas’ London Marathon win; Blazymania cruises into Biarritz — plus more industry moves that hit, and missed, the mark.
• @Adidas scored a big PR win at the London Marathon when two runners in its new Pro Evo 3 supershoe made history by completing the race in under two hours, finishing first and second while the top runner in Nike came in third, one day before the shoe’s North American release.
• Matthieu Blazy's first cruise show for @ChanelOfficial fuelled growing 'Blazymania' as the French couture giant cemented the success of its creative revamp.
• @Vinted hit an €8 billion valuation in a secondary share sale, up 60 percent from the €5 billion valuation it achieved in a similar 2024 transaction, as resale continues to grow.
• @Prada Group reported modest growth, missing analyst estimates as momentum at once-explosive @MiuMiu fell back down to earth.
• @SwanBeauty_US’ skin-analysing mirror was all over social media, thanks to its shrewd sponsorship of influencer Brigette Pheloung's (@acquired.style) lavish bachelorette party.
• India’s Reliance Retail is aiming to scale Priyanka Chopra Jonas' (@PriyankaChopra) hair-care brand @AnomalyHaircare after acquiring it for an undisclosed sum.
• “The Devil Wears Prada 2” accurately chronicles the decline of traditional fashion media — but lacks the punch of the original.
This Week in Fashion is BoF’s weekly news briefing, available exclusively to BoF Professional members. Become a BoF Professional member to access the full analysis #linkinbio

posts.caption:
This editorial presents Anja Rubik and Ibrahim Kamara in a poetic, gender-fluid visual dialogue for the May 2020 collaboration between Vogue Italia and L’Uomo Vogue. Photographed by Harley Weir, the story is built around the idea of mirroring: masculine and feminine codes reflect, overlap, and dissolve into one another. Vogue Italia describes the project as two complementary fashion stories across the women’s and men’s magazines, exploring gender in fashion and society through “a game of mirrors.”
The images feel intimate, theatrical, and quietly radical. Rubik and Kamara appear as both individuals and alter egos, moving through sharply styled tailoring, sculptural silhouettes, exposed skin, headpieces, and graphic beauty details. Kamara’s styling uses fashion as a language of transformation, placing classic menswear, couture-like gestures, and body-conscious styling into a space where identity becomes fluid rather than fixed. The result is less a conventional fashion editorial than a visual conversation about self-recognition, performance, and becoming.
Harley Weir’s photography gives the story a dreamlike tension: soft yet direct, sensual yet cerebral. The editorial’s strength lies in its balance between vulnerability and control. Rubik’s presence is fierce and statuesque, while Kamara brings a personal, performative energy that makes the story feel like an exchange between artist, stylist, model, and subject. Together, they create an editorial that treats gender not as a binary, but as a living, shifting surface—something seen, styled, reflected, and reimagined.
Credits: Photography: Harley Weir. Styling/Fashion Editor: Ibrahim “Ib” Kamara. Models: Anja Rubik and Ibrahim Kamara. Make-up: Hiromi Ueda, using Le Volume Stretch de Chanel and Boy de Chanel Le Teint. Hair: Cyndia Harvey, using Oribe. Manicure: Sylvie MacMillan. Set Design: Alice Kirkpatrick. Bondage Artists: Anna Barros and Erlend Davidson at Anatomie Studio. On Set: Shiny Projects. Stylist Assistant: Sasha Harris. Casting Director: Piergiorgio Del Moro. Editors: Emanuele Farneti and Thomas Persson. Publications: Vogue Italia and L’Uomo Vogue, May 2020.

posts.caption:
Born in Jamaica and raised between strict religion, rebellion, and reinvention, she became one of those rare figures who made Fashion feel bigger than just clothing. In the 1970s, she moved through New York and Paris as a model, but she never fit the soft, pretty mold the industry expected from women at that time. That was the point. Her beauty was sharp, sculptural, and impossible to soften.
Grace turned her body into architecture. The flat-top hair, the power shoulders, the dark sunglasses, the sharp tailoring, the metallic makeup. She played with masculinity and femininity before fashion had comfortable language for it. One moment she looked like a goddess, the next like a nightclub villain, the next like a living sculpture.
What made her different was that she didn’t wear fashion to look desirable. She wore it to look powerful. Designers, photographers, and artists didn’t just dress her, they built worlds around her. With Jean-Paul Goude, her image became surreal, graphic, almost superhuman. With designers like Azzedine Alaïa, her strength, shape, and confidence became part of the clothes themselves.
Her life moved between music, modeling, film, art, and performance, but fashion was always the thread connecting everything. She made disco feel dangerous, androgyny feel luxurious, and glamour feel untouchable.
Grace Jones didn’t become a fashion icon because she followed style.
She became one because she made style obey her.

posts.caption:
The 80s remain one of fashion’s most recognizable references, not because of nostalgia alone, but because the decade understood impact. In „Collections“ for Vogue Japan March 2018, Luigi & Iango bring that energy into a contemporary context: sharp shoulders, rich textures, bold styling and a sense of glamour that feels confident.
The editorial plays with classic 80s codes without making them look like costume. Anna Dello Russo’s styling mixes structure, shine, lace, leather and exaggerated silhouettes in a way that feels controlled and editorial. The result is dramatic, but never messy — a reminder that excess can still be precise.
Hair and makeup complete the mood with volume, definition and attitude, while the models carry the clothes with the kind of presence the era demands. It feels powerful, visual and slightly theatrical, but still refined. This is why the 80s continue to influence fashion: they gave designers permission to be bold, and editorials like this show how modern that boldness can still feel.

Magazine: Vogue Japan, March 2018
Photographers: Luigi & Iango
Stylist: Anna Dello Russo
Models: Kiki Willems, Lexi Boling, Luna Bijl, Paul Hameline, Selena Forrest, Zhengyang Zhang

posts.caption:
Absent Findings is one of those young fashion brands that seems to exist just outside the usual noise. It has shown in Milan and Dubai Fashionweek, appeared in publications like Vogue Italia, and built real industry credibility. Still, it has not fully crossed into wider fashion awareness.
That is what makes it interesting.
Founded by Shivin Singh, the Dubai-based design studio works with memory, craft, and reconstruction. Its clothes combine South Asian textile references, European tailoring, vintage saris, surplus fabrics, and hand-finished details. The result feels considered, slightly raw, and personal rather than loud or trend-driven.
The brand’s world is built through wrapped tops, loose trousers, sculptural bags, layered fabrics, muted desert tones, and garments that feel lived-in. In its imagery, models stand in open landscapes, quiet architecture, and desert spaces. The clothes are not styled for obvious impact; they sit naturally inside the atmosphere.
On paper, Absent Findings is already more established than many people might assume. It has presented during Milan Fashion Week, shown at Dubai Fashion Week, and appeared at Lineapelle Designers Edition and Fondazione Sozzani. Press from Vogue, Vogue Italia, Vanity Fair, and other titles adds to that credibility.
What makes the brand stand out is that it still feels understated. The references are visible, but not over-explained. South Asian craft and textile memory are present without feeling decorative. European tailoring is there too, but the clothes remain soft, personal, and slightly offbeat.
Even with the social proof, Absent Findings still feels like something you discover rather than something being pushed at you. In a fashion landscape where young labels often become overexposed quickly, this one seems to be building slowly and with intention.
Maybe that is why it is worth paying attention to now.

posts.caption:
Even though we all already know Lady Diana’s style was amazing, it still deserves to be talked about.
Her style was influential because it felt both royal and real. She could wear a glamorous gown with diamonds one day, then a sweatshirt, biker shorts, and sneakers the next, and somehow both looks became iconic. Diana understood fashion as a form of communication. Her outfits showed confidence, softness, rebellion, elegance, and freedom,  sometimes all at once.
What made her style so cool was that it never felt stuck in one image. She evolved from polished princess looks to bolder, more independent fashion choices, and people followed that transformation closely. The oversized blazers, statement collars, sleek evening dresses, pearls, casual sportswear, and that unforgettable “revenge dress” all became part of her visual legacy.
Lady Diana’s influence is still everywhere today. Celebrities, designers, and fashion lovers continue to reference her looks because they feel timeless but also effortless. She proved that style is not just about looking perfect, it is about owning who you are, even when the whole world is watching.

posts.caption:
Amidst a sea of lavish gowns at the Met Gala, nearly all of which were custom pieces crafted by the most prolific fashion houses on the planet, one look stood out for the exact opposite reason; Bhavitha Mandava appeared to be wearing a casual quarter-zip with straight cut blue jeans.
To be fair, there’s more to the look than meets the eye. First of all, the outfit is Chanel and it is, technically, an illusion made from silk, not denim. Second, it calls back to the outfit Mandava, a grad student at NYU, was wearing on the day she entered the Atlantic Avenue subway station and was ‘discovered’ by a modeling agent. She became a bright star in the modeling world at a meteoric pace, and in March of this year became the first Indian woman to hold a coveted Chanel ambassadorship.
But, the Met Gala look isn’t the first time Chanel has reinterpreted Mandava’s fated subway station look; she opened Chanel’s Pre-Fall ‘26 show, which was held on a defunct NYC subway platform, in a recreation of the same outfit. Why, then, was it necessary to churn the same look out at such an important event as the Met Gala?
Some fans, especially those with Indian and South Asian heritage, are enraged, calling the outfit “racist” and “a microaggression” and pointing out that the outfit would not have been given to a white model or ambassador. IG fashion commentator Sufi Motiwala said he was “pissed” and accused Chanel of “tokenizing” Mandava.
“Be serious,” said Anisha Ramakrishna Tarpara on IG, “the outfit to get on the subway, the outfit I wear to go to CVS. Chanel really said ‘we see India and we want to meet you underground.’”
The overly casual look which the design house has prioritized for their first ever Indian ambassador seems to peddle a narrative which excludes her from the glamour of her counterparts. Mandava’s Chanel counterparts, by the way, include Jennie, Margot Robbie, Lily Rose-Depp, Gracie Abrams, and Anna Wintour herself who each wore luxurious evening gowns to the Gala. As did Awar Odhiang, a fellow Chanel model of comparable viral fame. In a bathroom selfie taken by Mandava, the discrepancy of her jeans-and-a-casual-top outfit is impossible to ignore.
Thoughts?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ™ on May 05, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'Was Chanel's Met Gala Look for Bhavitha Mandava Racist? Chanel dressed their first-ever Indian ambassador in an extremely casual look for fashion's biggest night. Diet PRADA'.

Second, it calls back to the outfit Mandava, a grad student at NYU, was wearing on the day she entered the Atlantic Avenue subway station and was ‘discovered’ by a modeling agent. She became a bright star in the modeling world at a meteoric pace, and in March of this year became the first Indian woman to hold a coveted Chanel ambassadorship.

But, the Met Gala look isn’t the first time Chanel has reinterpreted Mandava’s fated subway station look; she opened Chanel’s Pre-Fall ‘26 show, which was held on a defunct NYC subway platform, in a recreation of the same outfit. Why, then, was it necessary to churn the same look out at such an important event as the Met Gala?

Some fans, especially those with Indian and South Asian heritage, are enraged, calling the outfit “racist” and “a microaggression” and pointing out that the outfit would not have been given to a white model or ambassador. IG fashion commentator Sufi Motiwala said he was “pissed” and accused Chanel of “tokenizing” Mandava.   

“Be serious,” said Anisha Ramakrishna Tarpara on IG, “the outfit to get on the subway, the outfit I wear to go to CVS. Chanel really said ‘we see India and we want to meet you underground.’”

The overly casual look which the design house has prioritized for their first ever Indian ambassador seems to peddle a narrative which excludes her from the glamour of her counterparts. Mandava’s Chanel counterparts, by the way, include Jennie, Margot Robbie, Lily Rose-Depp, Gracie Abrams, and Anna Wintour herself who each wore luxurious evening gowns to the Gala. As did Awar Odhiang, a fellow Chanel model of comparable viral fame. In a bathroom selfie taken by Mandava, the discrepancy of her jeans-and-a-casual-top outfit is impossible to ignore.

Thoughts?

posts.caption:
It’s the first Monday in May! What will you be up to this evening? Chances are, you won’t be at the Met… at least not physically. Chances are you’ll be there in spirit and on Instagram, as is the tradition on this storied holiday for fashion folks and pop culture followers.
But this year feels a little different because many are calling for a boycott of the Gala. Jeff Bezos and his wife Lauren Sanchez are sponsors and co-chairs this year (not exactly for the first time, as Jeff was an honorary chair in 2012) which feels like it crosses a line for audiences who have, in years past, interacted with the festivities despite a myriad of controversies. Who can forget the anxiety surrounding 2015’s “China: Through The Looking Glass” theme? Or the debates about Karl Lagerfeld’s history of troubling remarks when he was honored in 2023? This year, because of Bezos, protests are being planned and ad hoc demonstrations have already been cropping up all over NYC, courtesy of a group called “Everyone Hates Elon”.
How does that affect you? The audiences at home? Are you still planning to keep up with the outfits? Will you post about your favorite (and maybe also your least favorite) stars? Afterall, the Gala exists to benefit the Met’s Costume Institute. Many online are pointing out that supporting the arts is net positive, regardless of where the money is coming from. The costume institute is, afterall, the only wing of the Met that must fund itself.
Others, though, are exhausted by the state of the economy and the price of gas and groceries. And, in light of the wars our country is participating in overseas, not everyone is able to justify participating in such a flashy show of wealth and celebrity. That’s saying nothing of their distrust (and disgust) for tech industry billionaires and their growing resentment towards tech’s involvement in fashion.
So, is ‘boycotting’ the #MetGala a slap in the face of all the work the institute does to preserve garments with profound historical significance? Or is it an honest refusal of tech oligarchy that can help shape Met Galas in years to come?
The jury is out. How will you be engaging (or not) with the Gala this evening?

posts.caption:
Condé Nast recently announced layoffs following a substantial reduction in force just last year. In February, The Washington Post’s staff was all but gutted by Jeff Bezos. The BBC, CBS, and Politico have undergone similar cuts as well. This epidemic of slashed newsrooms is precisely the issue ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ set its sights on. For the audience, these issues are all too real.
But, does this long-awaited sequel have the same grit as its 2006 predecessor to really honor the issues it tackles?
Spoiler: It does not.
What it does have is a bogey man called Big Tech. Where DWP1 showed audiences the challenging and ethically dubious business of fashion, DWP2 depicts the tech bro takeover of the fashion world. Nigel quips that Runway is no longer a magazine but is a content machine for people to scroll “while they pee.” And the film’s villains are a pair of devil-may-care Silicon Valley chads a la the Bezos’, Elon Musk, and Bryan Johnson, who believe that the content and operations of ‘Runway’ can be replaced by AI.
What, then, does the film offer as an antidote to a media landscape full of deepfakes and slop? Not only are entire scenes self-referential but they’re literally identical to the first film. Sure, the hallmark DWP outfit montages deliver a few thrills, but they feel awkwardly mimetic and aware of themselves. And, while the lead actors’ charming chemistry holds the film together, they are forced to deliver predictable dialogue in the exact cadence they did twenty years ago. Because of this uncanny duplicity, the film fails to demonstrate the very innovation that it begs for.
As a result, DWP2 feels like a fairytale for a generation of fashion and media professionals who have long grown out of fairytales. It insists that a bootstraps work ethic plus a strong moral compass will win you your wildest dreams, despite all the rich and powerful oppressors you may encounter on the way. But we were told that same tale in 2006… and perhaps that one is better left to nostalgia?

Lights, camera, @billieeilish.
At just 24 years old, the 10-time Grammy winner and two-time Oscar winner has existed in the public consciousness for a decade now. But this May, a new concert documentary attempts to capture her star power and stage presence in a new light—through the eyes of legendary director James Cameron, no less. Eilish never thought she’d make another documentary after 2021’s ‘Billie Eilish: The World’s a Little Blurry,’ which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. “I’m so private about my actual life now,” Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‘Avatar’ filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up—and co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

To interpret the “Fashion Is Art” dress code at Monday’s 2026 #MetGala, @eileengu looked to an untraditional source: bubbles.
While many channeled works of art on the red carpet, the Olympic gold medalist turned herself into a literal performance piece. Designed by @irisvanherpen, Gu wore a one-of-a-kind mini “Airo” dress made from 15,000 glass globes—a design that would only to be taken to the next level on the red carpet, where constellations of floating bubbles drifted into the air as she posed.
“I love the way that it speaks to motion and time,” Gu told ELLE before the gala. “As an athlete, I’m constantly in motion, so things that feel so quick can suddenly freeze. When I’m in the air, it’s as if time slows down.”
At the link in bio, get your inside look at the design process and Gu’s preparation for the first Monday in May.

While naked dressing has long been a hallmark of the first Monday in May, this year, the risqué style of dress felt especially on theme. The gala’s broad dress code “Fashion Is Art” invited guests to literally turn themselves into walking canvases. And while many channeled painterly techniques, others turned themselves—and their bodies—into wearable works of art. posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

posts.caption:
Is it surprising to anyone that this year’s Met Gala was a parade of excess? It shouldn’t have been 🙃⁠
⁠
Fashion has *always* profited from exploitation and, as billionaires prove, wealth inequality in this industry and beyond are exactly why a handful at the top in American and Europe get rich as workers worldwide suffer. If this doesn’t push you toward ethical fashion, then what will?⁠
⁠
We needn’t remind you that more sustainable fashion isn’t just about the environment but a holistic rethinking of the structural issues the industry has long profited off of. More sustainable fashion means all involved are cared for, from those who make your clothes to the communities affected by clothing production 💚⁠
⁠
What are your thoughts on this year’s Met Gala and it’s proximity to wealth? Is this business as usual? A new low? A searing call to action? Share in the comments, and let this be a reminder to explore top rated brands, small, and independent brands quality assured by our team of experts: explore the latest, at the link ↑⁠

posts.caption:
Fashion Is Art — and last night two of its greatest creative directors made their point on the biggest stage of all. Stevie Nicks, 77, making her first ever Met Gala appearance, wore John Galliano for Zara: midnight-blue silk taffeta, a velvet jacket, a top hat. Moody, witchy, brilliant, and very her.
Meanwhile Kendall Jenner wore Gap. Not just any old Gap, this was GapStudio by Zac Posen, a sculptural draped gown constructed from actual Gap white T-shirts manipulated into liquid marble, inspired by the Winged Victory of Samothrace, goddess of victory. In a T-shirt. On the steps of the Met.
High street just took its seat at the top table. And, given all the top designers now embedded in high street brands, from Jonathan Saunders at & Other Stories to Clare Waight Keller at Uniqlo, it’s never going to give those seats back.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Grazia UK on May 05, 2026. May be an image of magazine, poster, gown and text that says 'High Street brands on the Met steps? You bet! GR GRAZIA'.

posts.caption:
Last night, Lauren Sánchez Bezos walked the Met Gala carpet solo as Jeff Bezos was MIA – until later in the evening when he slipped in backstage – presumably to avoid protestors and paparazzi.
Even Sánchez Bezos was notably low-key in Schiaparelli couture – the navy-blue body-con number with pearl straps was said to be inspired by John Singer Sargent. Bezos, meanwhile when spotted later at the party, didn’t even attempt the ‘Fashion Is Art’ theme, opting for the most normal black-tie tuxedo. But then he gets to dress like a James Bond villain if he wants to because he wasn’t merely a guest - he paid for it. $10 million, to be precise, the largest individual financial commitment in Met Gala history.
Protest posters plastered across Manhattan read: ‘The Bezos Met Gala - brought to you by worker exploitation.’ New York’s democratic socialist mayor sent his regrets and spent the evening celebrating garment workers instead.
The fashion industry looked on – glared? – with genuine anxiety about what happens when the most powerful night in fashion is answerable not to an editor, curator or designer, but to a man whose primary business is logistics.
Art, it seems, is only as free as whoever’s paying for it. Tap the link in bio for the full piece.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Grazia UK on May 05, 2026. May be a meme of one or more people, magazine and text that says 'This Met Gala was brought to you by Amazon Art is only as free as whoever's paying for it GRAZIA'.

posts.caption:
Fringing! Sequins! Corsets! Crowns!
The dress code was ‘Fashion Is Art’. And like any gallery worth visiting, the range was extraordinary – from masterwork fibre glass corsets (here’s looking at you Kim Kardashian in full Allan Jones pop art corset) to baffling concept pieces (hi Katy Perry in puzzling tarot card theme). Chase Infiniti arrived in 1 million sequins as the Venus de Milo courtesy of Thom Browne, trailing a fringe train in over 600 colours. Madonna ascended the Met steps in Anthony Vaccarello’s Saint Laurent – a long black cloak and sheer cape ferried by seven ladies in waiting, and let’s not forget a full armada arranged on her head.
Meanwhile, Alex Consani wore a dress drawn directly from Botticelli’s Primavera, thanks to Demna’s Gucci. Beyoncé was a literal skeleton of crystals, merci Olivier Rousteing and Sarah Paulson wore a Matières Fécales ballgown with a $1 bill taped across her eyes – the outfit was titled ‘The One Per Cent’, a scathing commentary or not-too-subtle protest? It was the only truly political statement on a carpet that could have made many more.
And finally Rihanna, cool as a cucumber in Masion Margiela. Nine minutes after close. As is tradition. Art, darlings…

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is the real deal. For three decades, Sevigny has cut a singular path, both on screen and in fashion, building a body of work that feels as unexpected as it does authentically her own. In Bazaar’s May 2026 issue, the actor talks about her next move—“The Five-Star Weekend,” a drama series adapted from the Elin Hilderbrand novel in which she stars alongside Jennifer Garner. It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that’s precisely why it also makes sense; she’ll never fit neatly into any box. “My agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‘This is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,’” Sevigny says, “but I really feel there’s really something for everybody.”

posts.caption:
A @cartier Crash just sold for about $2 million, setting a new record and continuing a sharp climb from previous sales in 2022 and 2021. Once a niche oddball, the Crash has gone fully mainstream, thanks in part to fans like Tyler, the Creator, Jay-Z, and Timothée Chalamet. Its warped, surreal shape has even spilled beyond watches into fashion and sneaker culture, turning it into one of Cartier’s most in-demand pieces.⁠
⁠
Now Cartier is doubling down. At Watches & Wonders 2026, it unveiled a new skeletonized Crash limited to just 150 pieces, stripping the design back to expose its inner mechanics in full. It’s the boldest version yet, and if auction prices are anything to go by, the Crash’s legend isn’t cooling off anytime soon.

posts.caption:
The Japanese label has carved out a sweet spot where oversized silhouettes meet high-performance tech, and people can’t get enough. From WINDSTOPPER shirts to GORE-TEX jackets with +Phenix, every drop vanishes almost instantly. The formula works because it flips expectations: classic pieces like oxfords and tees look familiar but are built with COOLMAX fibers, dry-touch polyester, and adjustable details that actually do something. Even the exaggerated “mega” cargos balance their bulk with real functionality like airflow-friendly mesh lining.⁠
⁠
Led by Ryo Takashima, @the_clesste taps into that “city boy” ideal, remixing wardrobe staples through volume, fabric innovation, and subtle utility. Nothing feels overdesigned, nothing is overpriced, and everything just lands. Big fits, bigger ideas, and a brand that clearly knows why it’s winning.

posts.caption:
@wisdm’s ascent to being crowned the internet’s fashion king has been visible in real time, even for those who aren’t chronically online. On any given day, he’s building outfits from the most unexpected concepts, often crowdsourced directly from his audience. So when he made his Met Gala debut in 2024, it felt like the fashion gods finally got it right. When he wasn’t invited back in 2025, it raised questions. Wisdom, however, kept it classy, tweeting, “I was not invited to the Met, I know you guys wanna see me there so just letting y’all know. Looking forward to seeing all the beautiful interpretations of black dandyism.”
When we caught up with Wisdom Kaye ahead of his return at The Mark Hotel, he spoke about making his comeback with @publicschoolnyc, who have also recently returned to the runway. “When you take time away and come back, there’s something to prove, something you’ve taken time to master,” he tells us.
Scroll through for a glimpse into the madness, then head to the link in bio for the full story.
Photo: @sarahschecker / Hypebeast

posts.caption:
Music is never out of @maluma’s mind — not even at the Met Gala ✨🇨🇴⁠
⁠
The Colombian superstar was the first Latin artist to walk the carpet at last night’s Met Gala, arriving in a custom @tomford look that fully embraced the spotlight. Even on fashion’s biggest night, his upcoming seventh studio album ‘Loco X Volver’ remained front and center ahead of its May 15 release.⁠
⁠
For his fifth Met Gala appearance, Maluma also turned the focus toward his team through his “We Made This” Instagram campaign, honoring the creatives working behind the scenes. From his debut in 2019 to becoming a staple on the carpet, Maluma continues carrying his Latin identity with him through every flashbulb moment.⁠
⁠
We spoke with Maluma about ‘Loco X Volver’, his Met Gala experience, and the world finally getting to know Juan Luis.⁠
⁠
Photo: @padox__/Getty Images⁠
⁠
---⁠
⁠
La música nunca sale de la mente de @maluma — ni siquiera en la Met Gala ✨🇨🇴⁠
⁠
La superestrella colombiana fue el primer artista latino en pisar la alfombra de la gala de anoche, llegando con un look custom de @tomford que abrazó por completo el spotlight. Incluso en la noche más importante de la moda, su próximo séptimo álbum de estudio, 'Loco X Volver', siguió siendo protagonista antes de su lanzamiento el 15 de mayo.⁠
⁠
Para su quinta aparición en la Met Gala, Maluma también puso el foco en su equipo a través de su campaña de Instagram “We Made This”, reconociendo a los creativos que trabajan detrás de escena. Desde su debut en 2019 hasta convertirse en una figura habitual de la alfombra, Maluma sigue llevando su identidad latina consigo en cada flash.⁠
⁠
Hablamos con Maluma sobre 'Loco X Volver', su experiencia en la Met Gala y el mundo finalmente conociendo a Juan Luis.⁠

posts.caption:
The quietest looks are always the loudest statements, and Avneet Kaur has known this for a while now. 🌸
Draped in the deep, unhurried cool of monochromatic indigo, she doesn’t chase the moment. She sets the pace for it. Denim-on-denim, lace against leather, coastal light and shadow, every element dialled into a frequency that’s entirely her own.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this isn’t just a fashion story. It’s a portrait of a generation that leads with composure, one that finds its power not in the loudest room, but in the stillest one. ✨
The industry moves fast. Avneet doesn’t rush. And somehow, she’s always exactly where the moment needs her to be.
Cool, collected, and completely in control, this is the indigo hour. And it belongs to her.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.

posts.caption:
Jaguar Land Rover India has sharply revised pricing for the Range Rover SV, cutting up to ₹75 lakh ahead of the India–UK Free Trade Agreement rollout.
The flagship SUV now starts at ₹3.5 crore (ex-showroom), down from ₹4.25 crore, marking a reduction of nearly 18 percent. The move extends to the Range Rover Sport SV as well, which sees a ₹40 lakh drop, now priced at ₹2.35 crore. ￼
This pre-emptive price correction reflects expected duty benefits under the upcoming trade agreement, which will significantly lower import tariffs on UK-built completely built units. The revised pricing is already in effect, positioning these high-end SV models more competitively within India’s luxury SUV space. ￼
Notably, the adjustment applies only to UK-imported SV variants, while locally assembled Range Rover models and vehicles produced outside the UK remain unaffected. ￼

The Met Gala may be fashion’s most exclusive night, but not every celebrity is part of it, either by choice or circumstance. According to Cosmopolitan, only one person has been officially banned by Anna Wintour, while several others have chosen to stay away over the years.
Donald Trump remains the only publicly confirmed ban, while figures like Zayn Malik have openly rejected the event. Others, including Tina Fey and Amy Schumer, have criticised the gala’s culture and opted not to return.
The list highlights how the Met Gala’s exclusivity is shaped not just by invitations, but also by who chooses to step away.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

Manish Malhotra’s (@manishmalhotra05) muses translate art, memory, and craft into couture at the Met Gala 2026.
From Karan Johar’s (@karanjohar) Raja Ravi Varma-inspired ensemble to Camila Mendes (@camimendes) channelling Amrita Sher-Gil, and Sudha Reddy’s (@sudhareddy.official) Kalamkari-rooted Tree of Life outfit, each look unfolds as a distinct narrative shaped through Indian craftsmanship.
Even Dwayne Johnson’s (@therock) emerald brooch becomes part of the larger language, where jewellery and textile move in sync.
Across the carpet, Malhotra’s vision positions couture not just as adornment, but as authorship, placing Indian craft firmly at the centre of the global fashion conversation.

posts.caption:
Sudha Reddy (@sudhareddy.official) in Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotraworld) at the Met Gala 2026.
Returning to the Met in a custom ‘Tree of Life’ couture ensemble, the philanthropist anchors her look in a 550-carat tanzanite, layered with over USD 15 million worth of private jewellery, bringing scale, rarity, and personal legacy into sharp focus.
The gown reinterprets the ancient Kalamkari tradition through a sculptural, contemporary lens. Built on a corseted velvet base, it unfolds into an intricate narrative of zari, zardozi, and resham work, tracing motifs from Telangana’s cultural landscape across velvet, silk, and tulle.
An expansive train and sheer embroidered cape extend the story, while a sculptural back installation of the Kalpavriksha transforms the look into a moving tableau of craft and mythology.
Executed over 3,459 hours by more than 90 artisans, the ensemble positions couture as narrative, where textile, technique, and jewellery come together as one.
Styled by: Mariel Haenn (@marielhaenn)
#SudhaReddy #ManishMalhotra #MetGala2026 #IndianCouture

posts.caption:
Isha Ambani in Gaurav Gupta Couture (@gauravguptaofficial) at the Met Gala 2026.
A saree, reimagined as a complete work of art.
At the centre, a jewellery-integrated blouse set with over 1,800 carats of diamonds, emeralds, polki, and kundan, drawing from Nita Ambani’s personal collection. Constructed over 500 hours by 40 artisans, the piece merges ornament directly into textile through zardozi, dabka, and nakshi techniques, blurring the line between jewellery and garment.
Paired with a custom gold-woven tissue saree by the Swadesh collective, worn in its traditional drape, and a sculptural cape developed through Gaurav Gupta’s signature resin technique, the look traces a lineage of over 5,000 years of Indian craft.
Hand-painted and embroidered motifs inspired by the Ajanta cave murals anchor the narrative, while a historic Mughal-era sarpech from the Nizams of Hyderabad completes the ensemble.
Over 50 artisans. More than 1,200 hours. One continuous story of Indian craftsmanship on a global stage.
Styled by: Anaita Shroff Adajania (@anaitashroffadajania)
#IshaAmbani #GauravGupta #MetGala2026 #IndianCouture #Swadesh

American actress Camila Mendes (@camimendes), known for her role as Veronica in the hit series Riverdale, arrives at the #MetGala2026 in a mahogany custom couture gown by Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotraworld), that frames fashion as art in motion.
Inspired by the women in Amrita Sher-Gil’s paintings, Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05) reinterprets the artist’s introspective portraits through couture- translating their quiet intensity and presence into form.
The gown, structured through a corseted base and hand-ruched in French chiffon, gathers at the hip in a sculptural knot before cascading into a fluid trail. Its surface evokes the texture of dry brushstrokes, rendered in a deep mahogany hue drawn from her palette of Indian reds, browns, and ochres, lending depth and movement to the silhouette.
Completed with high jewellery in tourmalines, uncut diamonds, and 18k gold from Manish Malhotra High Jewellery (@manishmalhotrajewellery), the look holds its ground as a study in texture, tone, and presence- where couture meets canvas.
@metmuseum @metcostumeinstitute

posts.caption:
Long after the photographers had left the green cobblestone carpet of the Met Gala, celebrities filed into after-parties across New York City. At Boom, at the top of the Standard in the Meatpacking District of Manhattan, the designer Gabriela Hearst co-hosted a party with the actress Grace Gummer. Guests like ASAP Rocky and Lindsey Vonn mingled while Questlove spun vintage Chaka Khan.
Across town, the movie director Baz Luhrmann and his wife, the costume designer Catherine Martin, hosted a celebration at their East Village bar, Monsieur. At the Lower East Side nightclub the Box, scantily dressed dancers in sky-high heels shimmied beneath a disco ball before the D.J. Diplo arrived for his set.
The New York Times reporters @jacobbernsteinnyc and @smbharh14 caught up with the stars as they hit the after-parties across the city. Tap the link in our bio to get a look at who was out and what they wore. Photos by @rebeccasmeyne, @amirbangs and @vnina

posts.caption:
The Met Gala steps are a stage where celebrities strive not merely to look pretty, but to create indelible, avant-garde fashion moments. The most memorable looks tend to commit to the often abstract theme and take risks not typically seen on award show red carpets. Sometimes that works; other times it ends in disaster.
This year, guests stepped onto a mossy-looking, trompe l’oeil cobblestone carpet surrounded by a backdrop that recalled a Monet canvas. It was fitting for the evening’s brief: “Fashion is art.” It may be a bit on the nose for an event whose raison d’être is to raise money for the Metropolitan Museum of Art’s fashion-focused Costume Institute.
Of all of the looks from the Met Gala, presented in no particular order, these were our unequivocal favorites. Tap the link in our bio to see all 15 looks. Photos by @vnina and @amirbangs

posts.caption:
At this year’s Met Gala, some famous public figures were hidden behind masks, blindfolds, headpieces and makeup that created the illusion of a marble statue or an aging man. You might not know it to look at them, but the night was a celebration of the body.
Guests including Rihanna and ASAP Rocky, Doja Cat, Beyoncé and her daughter Blue Ivy showed out on Monday night, wearing outfits that riffed on the idea of “the dressed body,” the focus of the new fashion exhibition. That included corsets, foam, barely-there Saint Laurent gowns and different styles of flowers.
Dressed as a marble statue was Heidi Klum, wearing a look inspired by the Veiled Vestal, a 19th-century sculpture by Raffaelle Monti. She planned the look for three months and it took her five hours to get ready, she said, adding that it was the best her body had felt at a Met Gala. “For once, my feet don’t hurt,” she said, wiggling her marble-like toes in flat sandals.
The New York Times reporter @s_evangelina and the photographer @landonnordeman caught up with guests at the gala’s cocktail party. Tap the link in our bio to step inside the fete and read more about how guests spent their night at the museum.

posts.caption:
As onlookers waited with bated breath to see how guests interpreted the Met Gala theme “Fashion Is Art,” this year’s event had hit a few milestones already. Having raised a record $42 million, the Costume Institute unveiled the Condé M. Nast Galleries in the museum’s Great Hall, where this year’s exhibit “Costume Art” is being shown. But before entry into the gallery, there is, of course, the fashion.
Heidi Klum arrived dressed as a statue, the kind you might find inside the Greek and Roman wing of the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Sabrina Carpenter wore a dress that was made from the film of the movie “Sabrina.” Colman Domingo's multi-colored ensemble by Valentino was reminiscent of the harlequins from Picasso’s Rose period.
The event was a family affair: Beyoncé, an official co-chair, walked the carpet in an Olivier Rousteing gown, accompanied by her daughter Blue Ivy and husband Jay-Z, and Nicole Kidman, another co-chair, arrived with her daughter Sunday Rose Kidman Urban.
Reporters and photographers for @nytstyle covered the cobblestone carpet live as attendees made their arrivals. Tap the link in our bio to see all of the looks from fashion's biggest night. Photos by @vnina and @amirbangs

posts.caption:
It has become something of a Met Gala tradition for Rihanna to keep everyone waiting — and guessing about whether or not she will actually show — until not just the last minute, but way past the last minute.
She arrived on the carpet at 9:15 p.m. with ASAP Rocky. Rihanna wore a sculptural Maison Margiela gown.
“It’s giving oyster,” Rihanna said of her dress. She said it was reflective of her Caribbean heritage. Her favorite part of ASAP Rocky’s look was “the pink — I was never a pink girl,” she said, until she had a daughter.

posts.caption:
At first glance, #CharliXCX’s Saint Laurent #MetGala dress may appear boring? but a closer look at the details reveals a much deeper artistic reference. The design nods to #YvesSaintLaurent’s love of irises, inspired by #VincentvanGogh’s painting Irises, created in 1889.
This influence became most visible in Saint Laurent’s Spring/Summer 1988 haute couture collection, often referred to as his “Van Gogh collection.” Rather than using floral motifs, he transformed irises into intricate works of art through fashion. Jackets were richly embroidered with beads and sequins, recreating the texture and movement of Van Gogh’s brushstrokes.
What do you think of her look - too safe or just right?

posts.caption:
For over 15 years, @raulavilainc has been the man tasked with transforming the Metropolitan Museum of Art for the #MetGala.
Each year, he interprets the theme and translates it into large-scale installations, lighting, and table settings. His designs often centre around dramatic focal points in the Great Hall. In 2019, he created a 25-foot-tall flamingo centrepiece comprised of 30,000 flowers. In 2018, he erected a rose recreation of a papal crown. In 2013, Ávila and his team flew in a chandelier from London and covered it with razor blades.
Which Met Gala interiors have been your favourite throughout the years?

posts.caption:
Before #MirandaPriestly came the real fashion devil: #Cruella, who thanks to oscar winning costume designer Anthony Powell, was redefined as a fashion and film icon in 1996.
Anthony was inspired by #hautecouture, particularly the work of #ThierryMugler. Sharp tailoring, bold silhouettes, and rich runway textures were pushed into parody to reflect #GlennClose’s camp and villainous character. For example, the red feather “flame” dress (see slide 6) uses intricate detailing to create the illusion that she is engulfed in rage. Some of the corsetry was so extreme that Glenn reportedly felt close to fainting during filming.
Famously, Glenn negotiated in her contract to keep all of Cruella’s costumes, something Disney later tried to reconsider after realizing just how expensive and elaborate the designs were. The lasting impact of these outfits shows how costume design has never been the same since.

posts.caption:
#Opinion | Standup comedian Kaneez Surka @kaneezsurka recently opened up about the "beauty of divorce" on a podcast by Aditi Chandanani @therapywith_aditivc. "Oh you MUST get divorced!" Kaneez quipped, getting Aditi all excited about 'life 2.0'. "When you get divorced, society just drops all expectations of you... they give up on you, and the giving up is the best part!" Kaneez expressed.
Divorce used to be a woman's cue to hide way from society; a label that crept into every introduction. She is just a woman anymore; she is a divorcee. But the women of 2026 are rewriting what it means to walk away from an unfulfilling marriage. They are refusing to carry endings like shame, turning divorce into a cultural statement. Earlier, divorce defined the woman. Now, the woman defines the divorce.
A woman who leaves an unhappy marriage disrupts a comforting myth - that self sacrifice is the highest form of femininity. When she says, “This is not working for me,” she exposes how many others are quietly tolerating what does not work for them either. And that can feel threatening to many.  But here is the truth women know in their bones. Divorce doesn't celebrate heartbreak; it is usually the aftermath of it. So why should women carry the shame of something that often requires immense courage? Why should they be the ones apologising for choosing peace?

Karen Kshiti Suvarna, 22, is all set for the premiere of her debut film, 'September 21' at the Cannes Film Festival. The film, releasing in both Hindi and Kannada, explores the journey of a person living with Alzheimer's disease and the life of their caregivers.
Alzheimer's is a neurodegenerative disorder causing severe memory loss, cognitive decline, and behavioural changes.
Suvarna has spoken about reframing the disorder through the care instead of cure. She also reportedly visited care centres in Bengaluru to research for this film.

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

The 2026 Met Gala delivered. The looks have been ranked, posted, screenshotted, judged.
This is not a round-up. Stylist Elixir is not about critique other stylists. It is about the work behind the work.
The stylists who built the looks. The assistants who carried the bags. The tailors who finished
the seams in hotel hallways. The glam teams who synced every detail. The PRs who held the
timeline together. The couriers who got the pieces there. The producers who made the
impossible run on time.
Most of them will not get tagged. Most of them will not be named in a caption. That does not make their work less real. The fittings still happened. The samples still got there. The look still landed.
Recognition should not be a luxury. It should reflect the effort that built the moment. To everyone who worked on this year’s gala, in front of the camera or behind it. We see you.
And to the talent and the stylists who tagged their teams properly today. Thank you. The credit does not just say who. It says this work was collaborative. That nothing on a red carpet builds itself.

posts.caption:
Tonight is the Met Gala.
It is also a night the industry is watching closely. Beyoncé returning to the Met for the first time in a decade. Anthony Vaccarello as host committee co-chair, with Saint Laurent sponsoring the exhibition. The biggest billionaire sponsorship in the gala’s history drawing the loudest protests. The question of whether this is still fashion’s biggest night.
But while all of that plays out in public, another version of tonight is happening behind the scenes.
The world will watch fifteen seconds of red carpet.
What it will not watch is the six weeks before it.
Pulls beginning two months out. Customs delays. Tailors waiting in hotel hallways. Samples arriving unfinished. Glam teams syncing with stylists across three time zones. Assistants prepping looks on three hours of sleep. Stylists making last-minute adjustments at the hotel as their client walks out the door.
Most of it will never be seen. Most of the people who made it happen will never be named.
This is what styling at this level actually looks like. It is not glamorous. It is not effortless. It is years of being trusted to deliver, packed into a single night.
To every stylist working tonight, every assistant carrying eight bags, every tailor hemming in a hallway, every glam artist syncing every detail, every publicist holding the timeline together. We see you.
The longer post is on Substack today, including a note on what Simone Biles said last week and what it means for every working stylist holding their rate this year. Link in bio.
Comment STYLIST to find out how Stylist Elixir helps fashion stylists build the kind of careers that get them on jobs like this.

posts.caption:
At the Met Gala 2026, fashion has been framed as art, and celebrities have showcased clothing as material, labour, and object.
Karan Johar (@karanjohar) in Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05) treats the body as canvas, with painted and embroidered surfaces drawing from Raja Ravi Varma, while Malhotra’s own bandhgala foregrounds labour, displaying the process as a part of the final piece.
Siblings Gauravi Kumari (@gauravikumari) and Padmanabh Singh in Prabal Gurung (@pachojaipur) have built directly from archive and atelier. Gauravi’s look incorporates her grandmother, Maharani Gayatri Devi’s chiffon sari into a couture structure that preserves its drape, while Padmanabh’s Phulghar coat, developed over 600+ hours in velvet with aari, zardozi, dabka and resham work, turns Rajasthani craft into surface and object.
Natasha Poonawalla (@natasha.poonawalla) in Dolce & Gabbana × Marc Quinn shifts fashion into sculpture, centring an orchid form as the primary object, with the body acting as its base.
By drawing on craft traditions, art-historical references, and material-led construction, these celebrities have positioned their presence as part of a larger exchange where fashion operates as a legitimate artistic language on an international stage. They’re not merely wearers of couture.

posts.caption:
Reliance Retail’s beauty ambitions are getting harder to miss. The company has acquired Anomaly, the haircare brand co-founded by Priyanka Chopra Jonas, along with its trademarks, brand assets and digital properties, giving it full ownership of the brand’s intellectual property and digital ecosystem.
Financial terms of the deal have not been disclosed. Chopra Jonas is expected to remain associated with the brand as creative director, with a focus on product development, innovation and brand strategy.
Launched in the US in 2021, Anomaly entered India the following year through Nykaa. But in an increasingly crowded beauty market, the brand failed to become a category-defining brand, proving celebrity beauty is no longer enough on its own.
The acquisition now moves the brand into Reliance Retail’s growing beauty system, which include Tira and Sephora India. Under Reliance, the bet is that scale, shelf space and visibility can do what a celebrity alone could not.
For Reliance, Anomaly is another celebrity-backed brand in the portfolio. But the real acquisition is influence over the shelf, the platform and the consumer journey.
The celebrity gets the headline. The real business happens at the shelf.

Her long-term partnership with Nike turned performance wear into something genuinely high fashion, blending technical precision with an almost editorial-level attention to detail. She was known for tailoring her looks to each tournament—navy tones on the clay at Roland Garros, Swarovski-studded dresses under the lights at the US Open—always aware of the setting, always intentional.

posts.caption:
Fashion is entering its legacy era. The pace hasn’t slowed, but the focus has shifted. Instead of chasing micro-trends, the industry is leaning into what lasts. Call it the institutional turn. Heritage, permanence, icons.
You see it everywhere. The global rollout of The Devil Wears Prada 2. Fifth Avenue physically transforming for the Met Gala under the “Costume Art” theme. Brands reinforcing their own mythology instead of trying to outrun the algorithm.
At Celine, it’s about building iconography through accessories that actually stick. At Bottega Veneta, it’s sculptural objects—pieces that feel closer to design than trend. And across the board, there’s a pivot toward personal narrative: collections, capsules, and campaigns that read more like memoir than marketing.
The message is clear. In a market saturated with content, the real flex is longevity. Less “what’s next.” More “what lasts.”
#FashionIndustry #LuxuryFashion #MetGala #Celine #BottegaVeneta

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

There’s no official prize for best dressed at the Met Gala, but if there were, Robert Wun(@robertwun) would’ve swept it. The London College of Fashion graduate founded his namesake label in 2014, later pivoting exclusively to couture. Today, he is known for combining razor-sharp tailoring with imaginative silhouettes and a cinematic, otherworldly approach to dressmaking. In January 2023, Wun made history as the first designer from Hong Kong to join the official Paris Haute Couture calendar as a guest member. He has since created custom couture looks for everyone from Adele and Ariana Grande to Lady Gaga and Priyanka Chopra.
It’s no surprise, then, that his fantastical creations ended up on some of the best-dressed guests at this year’s Met Gala. At the link in bio, we revisit each of his looks from the night. Spoiler alert: not a single miss.

Sewing Patterns

posts.caption: To give more context for those who may not know, the MET gala is a fundraiser for the costume department of the Metropolitan Museum of art in New York, "The MET." The costume department of the MET does not receive any taxpayer dollars, it is completely funded by private donation. As this is a charity event, they need to have attendees who can donate or raise big funds. By getting celebrities to come, they get a lot of media attention. It is an excellent formula for funding this branch of the museum. The Museums in the USA are funded by American citizens, financiers, captains of industry, and artists coming together as is the case with the MET. Everyone who lives in New York City can visit the Metropolitan museum of art for free. It is a fantastic museum, the building is is 2.2 million square feet and yet can only show a portion of it's inventory at one time. The museum has way more than paintings and dresses. It has sculptures, Persian rugs, various pieces of historical and religious art, and a glorious collection of furniture. Although America has many museums, most of them pale in comparison to what you see in Europe. But the MET is one of our crown jewels. It is very expensive to operate and preserve. This type of fundraiser helps to prevent tax burden on the citizens of New York. And yes, I love the MET.

posts.caption:
A few days ago I posted about Mariano Fortuny and promised a full post on Henriette, because she deserved the spotlight rather than the margin.
This is that post.
Everything here traces to a single document…a patent filed in Paris on 10 June 1909, held at the Marciana Library in Venice. Mariano filed it under his name because he was present and the deadline required it. Then he wrote a note on the same document, in his own handwriting, naming Henriette Negrin as the inventor.
That note sat in the archive for 112 years but the V&A only updated their catalogue labels in 2021.
Mariano’s acknowledgement was there from the beginning…it just took the institution a century to follow it.

posts.caption:
Yesterday I posted about the Indus Valley, where cotton was first threaded through copper beads in 6000 BCE, where the draped rectangle became the primary garment of one of the largest civilisations in the ancient world.
That logic travelled west through Mesopotamia, Egypt and Greece. In 1909 it arrived in a palazzo on the Grand Canal in the hands of a man who rowed himself across the Venetian lagoon every morning to make gowns that could not be dated.
Mariano Fortuny called himself a painter but else called him the Magician of Venice. He was severely allergic to horses, obsessed with electricity and spent his life reaching for a father he lost at three years old.
Peggy Guggenheim wore the same Delphos for decades. Isadora Duncan wore it to revive Classical Greek dance. Marcel Proust used it as a symbol of desire, memory, and lost time throughout one of the greatest novels ever written.
The Met @metmuseum chose it to announce this year’s theme. Fashion is Art and Fortuny already knew.
He was reaching for ancient Greece but the tradition he was reviving is older than Greece. It goes back to the Indus Valley and to the origin of cloth.
Part of an ongoing series on fashion, history, and whose stories get told.
Collage work by @juliewolfeartist
@metgalaofficial_ @metcostumeinstitute
